title: CHAPTER 7 —
ADVANCED PRESENTATION SKILLS
segments.body_html:
Start with animation, because it is the most abused tool in presentation software and also, occasionally, the most usefu

Chapter 7 — Advanced Presentation Skills — The useful version first. A slide about a three-stage process has three stages. If you put all three on the screen at on

beats.text: Start with animation, because it is the most abused tool in presentation software and also, occasionally, the most useful. The useful version first. A slide about a three-stage process has three stages. If you put all three on the screen at once, the audience reads ahead. They are on stage three while you are still talking about stage one. The information is technically there, but you have lost control of where attention is.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

WITH MEDIA.

Internet Dependency — Internet Dependency — comparingfile size impact applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: Video and audio introduce a new failure mode into presentations: they require more of the technical environment than static slides do. A static slide needs a projector and a signal. A video needs a projector, a signal, the right codec, a working audio connection, and sufficient processing power to play back smoothly. Each additional requirement is another place the chain can break. The first decision with any embedded media is embed versus link. Embedded media lives inside the presentation file itself.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE RELIABILITY PROBLEM WITH M

title: WHAT REHEARSAL
ACTUALLY REVEALS.
segments.body_html:
Most people treat rehearsal as practice — a way to get more comfortable with the material. That is a secondary benefit.

What Rehearsal Actually Reveals — The first thing rehearsal reveals is timing. A presentation that you read through in your head takes a certain amount of

beats.text: Most people treat rehearsal as practice , a way to get more comfortable with the material. That is a secondary benefit. The primary benefit of rehearsal is diagnostic. It reveals things you cannot see any other way. The first thing rehearsal reveals is timing. A presentation that you read through in your head takes a certain amount of time. A presentation that you speak aloud, at your actual pace, pausing when you would actually pause, slowing down for complex points, takes longer. Almost always longer.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · WHAT REHEARSAL ACTUALLY REVEAL

segments.section: THE ROOM IS PART OF THE PRESEN
title: THE ROOM IS PART
OF THE PRESENTATION.
segments.body_html:
—
CoveringText Legibility From The Back Of The Room — Covering: Text Legibility From The Back Of The Room — what it means applied
Color Accuracy On The Projector — Color Accuracy On The Projector — what it means applied to the chapter example.
Clicker Or Keyboard Functionality — Clicker Or Keyboard Functionality — what it means applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: The most consistent source of presentation failure is not bad slides or poor delivery. It is technical failure in the presenting environment , things that would have been caught if the presenter had spent fifteen minutes in the room before the audience arrived. Text that is readable on a laptop is often unreadable from the back of the room. The standard guidance is to stand at the back of the presenting room before you present and read every slide.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · THE ROOM IS PART OF THE PRESEN

segments.section: WHEN THE ROOM IS TWO PLACES AT
title: WHEN THE ROOM IS TWO
PLACES AT ONCE.
segments.body_html:
A hybrid presentation — some people in the room, some on a video call — is two presentations happening simultaneously, a

When the Room Is Two Places at Once — If you present only to the people in the room, the remote participants become passive observers of a conversation they a

beats.text: A hybrid presentation , some people in the room, some on a video call , is two presentations happening simultaneously, and the trap is treating it as one. If you present only to the people in the room, the remote participants become passive observers of a conversation they are not part of. They hear muffled responses from in-room participants. They miss the room's energy. They disengage.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · WHEN THE ROOM IS TWO PLACES AT

title: PUTTING IT
TOGETHER.
segments.body_html:
The chapter began with a rehearsal etymology — to go over again. Everything here is a version of that.

Putting It Together — You build an animation and you go over it again to see if it fires when you expect. You embed a video and you go over it

beats.text: The chapter began with a rehearsal etymology , to go over again. Everything here is a version of that. You build an animation and you go over it again to see if it fires when you expect. You embed a video and you go over it again on the actual projector to see if it plays. You write a presentation and you go over it aloud to discover that it runs eight minutes instead of six.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · PUTTING IT TOGETHER

title: CHAPTER SUMMARY
segments.body_html:
Animations earn their place when they control the reveal of information — preventing the audience from reading ahead, en

Chapter Summary — Embedded media is more reliable than linked media for live presentations. Embed video so it travels with the file and pl

beats.text: Animations earn their place when they control the reveal of information , preventing the audience from reading ahead, enforcing narrative sequence, making comparison visible. They fail when they decorate. The test is simple: remove the animation and ask if the slide becomes ambiguous. If no, delete the animation. Choose one trigger type (On Click for live control) and reduce click-counts by grouping elements that belong together. Embedded media is more reliable than linked media for live presentations.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CHAPTER SUMMARY

title: EXERCISES
segments.body_html: 7.1→7.2→7.3→7.4→7.5

beats.text: 7.1 (The animation test.) Open any presentation you have made or find a sample slide deck online. Identify every animation in the deck. For each animation, apply the chapter's test: remove it mentally and ask whether the slide becomes ambiguous. For animations that fail the test, name what they were trying to do and what they should be replaced with , either nothing, or a simpler, purposeful alternative. 7.2 (Embed vs.

title: AI WAYBACK
MACHINE.
segments.body_html:
Ask it to describe Cooper's late-career work on "information landscapes"what she was trying to build, and why it didn't catch on until the 2010s
Ask"Cooper went from book designer to media-lab researcher to professor without a formal computer science degree. What did
Add the constraint"Answer using one specific Cooper design — the MIT Press logo or one of her Bolt, Beranek and Newman posters — and what

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Muriel Cooper was working on the Visible Language Workshop at MIT, where she built the visual grammar of digital interfaces , typography in motion, information landscapes, the bridge between Bauhaus design and screen-based communication decades before most people had heard of advanced presentation design and visual communication. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · AI WAYBACK MACHINE

beats.text: Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML chart using a quantitative y-axis with a zero baseline, 4-6 labeled categories on the x-axis, direct value labels above marks, one primary red series and neutral supporting marks. The figure should represent: Two versions of the same three-stage process slide , left: all three stages visible simultaneously with audience attention arrows scattering across all three; right: only stage one visible with audience attention arrows focused on stage one , illustrating how reveal animation controls attention.
